title: Lemon curd
subtitle: Egg-yolk, butter, sugar, lemon — set over gentle heat.
excerpt: Egg yolks, sugar, butter, and the juice and zest of three lemons cooked patiently over a bain-marie until the curd coats the back of a spoon. Soft-set, glossy, sharp. Makes about two 340 ml jars.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), 'Lemon Cheese for Cheesecakes' is the prototype. The bain-marie method gives the gentle heat that stops the eggs scrambling; USDA / FDA guidance on egg pasteurisation supports holding at 70°C+ for safety.

recipe.freezeNotes: Curd freezes for 3 months in sealed jars; defrost in the fridge overnight. The texture stays glossy.
recipe.batchNotes: Double works in a wider bain-marie; the cook time stretches a few minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps four weeks in the fridge once jarred. The flavour deepens after two days.

recipeTools.notes: Sits in the saucepan without touching the water.

glossaryTerms.definition: A heat-buffer setup: a bowl set over (not in) a pan of simmering water. The water stays under boiling; the bowl receives gentle, even heat that protects delicate egg mixtures from scrambling.

content.text: Three large unwaxed lemons, zested fine and juiced. Five egg yolks beaten with caster sugar in a metal bowl. Cold butter cubed and ready on a plate. The bowl sits over a saucepan of barely-simmering water and the cook stirs steadily for 12 to 15 minutes until the curd thickens to coat the back of a spoon. Off the heat, the butter is whisked in piece by piece until the curd is glossy. Strained, jarred, chilled.

The whole recipe rests on heat control. Direct heat scrambles the yolks before they thicken; a bain-marie keeps the water under 95°C, and the bowl never goes above 80°C. Patient stirring brings the curd to the right consistency without curdling. If sweat appears on the cook's forehead, the heat is too high.
Half-fill a medium saucepan with water; bring to a bare simmer. A metal bowl that sits over the pan without touching the water is the
. The bottom of the bowl shouldn't touch the water.
Whisk {{eggs}} whole eggs, {{egg-yolks}} egg yolks, and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in the bowl until pale and combined. Whisk in the zest and juice of {{lemon}} of lemons.

Sit the bowl over the saucepan. Stir steadily with a wooden spoon or silicone spatula, reaching every corner of the bowl. After 4 to 5 minutes the mixture thickens and changes from translucent to opaque. Continue stirring for another 7 to 10 minutes until the curd coats the back of the spoon and a finger dragged across the back leaves a clear line.
Off the heat, in goes the butter

Take the bowl off the saucepan. Whisk in {{unsalted-butter}} of cold cubed butter, one piece at a time, letting each piece melt into the curd before adding the next. The curd turns glossy and slightly thickens further as it cools.
Pass the curd through a fine-mesh sieve into a jug; this lifts out any scrambled egg threads. Pour into sterilised jars while still warm. Seal. Cool, then refrigerate.

items.cause: Heat too high or bowl touching the water.
items.fix: Strain through the sieve to lift out the flecks. Next time, water at a bare simmer and bowl raised clear.

items.cause: Pulled off the heat too early.
items.fix: Return to the bain-marie and continue cooking. The thicken happens at around 75°C; patience is the only ingredient missing.

items.cause: Grated too deep and caught the white pith.
items.fix: Microplane just the yellow outer skin. Lighter touch the next time.
Add butter off the heat in small pieces; let each melt before adding the next.

content.text: Lemon curd is the Victorian tea-time darling, spread on hot scones with cream, swirled through a meringue pie, layered into trifles, or eaten by the teaspoon from the jar at midnight. Two jars from a Saturday afternoon's cooking — one to keep, one to give.
